bibliographic.citation: An Apology for Lollard Doctrines, Attributed to Wicliffe: Now First Printed from a Manuscript in the Library of Trinity College, Dublin, with an Introduction and Notes by James Henthorn Todd (London: Printed for the Camden Society by John Bowyer Nichols and Son, 1842), Camden Society Publications 20, lxiii, 206, [1] pp.
bibliographic.publisher: Printed for the Camden Society by John Bowyer Nichols and Son
bibliographic.title: An Apology for Lollard Doctrines, Attributed to Wicliffe

entities.label: Todd 1842 edition of An Apology for Lollard Doctrines
notes: Todd's introduction occupies pp. v–lxiii; the Middle English text pp. 1–113; notes pp. 115–188; glossary pp. 189–206; and an unnumbered errata leaf follows.
Todd calls the work the twenty-sixth article under his older 29-item description of MS 245. Jefferson's corrected modern description groups the same work as item 24 in a 27-item contents list.
The title and running title ‘Wicliffe's Apology’ were supplied by Todd and have no manuscript authority.

rights.basis: The edition was published in London in 1842. Internet Archive's item-specific evidence records no visible copyright notice and possible status NOT_IN_COPYRIGHT for the United States.
rights.component: The anonymous medieval text and Todd's 1842 edition, introduction, notes, glossary, and typographical presentation

entities.description: A first-half-fifteenth-century parchment Lollard miscellany written by one scribe. The Apology is one component at corrected fols. 163r–217r; older descriptions and the current catalog use old fols. 164–218 because the ink foliation skipped 133.

holding.notes: The exact current catalog record is local record CALM00230. Its old-foliation description identifies the Apology at fols. 164–218 by incipit and states ‘Not by Wycliffe.’

entities.label: Trinity College Dublin MS 245
notes: The corrected work locus is fols. 163r–217r. Todd's fol. 164a–218a and TCD's fols. 164–218 are legacy-foliation equivalents.

Jefferson's analysis places the scribe in the southern Central Midlands and most likely Huntingdonshire, with occasional more northerly forms possibly belonging to the exemplar or scribe. This does not establish the place of composition.
TCD MS 244 is separately digitized and must not be substituted merely because its shelfmark is adjacent.

physicalDescription.extent: Jefferson: iii + 218 + iii leaves; the older/current ink foliation appears to number 219 because it skips 133
physicalDescription.format: 159 × 108 mm; writing space 119 × 77 mm; one column of 32 lines
notes: Written in a single Anglicana hand with slight Secretary influence.
Blue initials with red decoration, red paraphs and touching, occasional red-underlined biblical quotations, and marginal corrections occur throughout the codex.
The post-medieval binding was rebacked in 1947.
Jefferson records likely sixteenth-century ownership by Thomas Chamber and transfer to the University of Dublin by Charles II.
physicalDescription.support: Parchment (described as membrane by Jefferson and vellum by the TCD catalog)

rights.basis: The text survives in a manuscript copied in the first half of the fifteenth century and no modern author is asserted; this finding does not cover modern photographs, catalog metadata, or provider contracts.
rights.component: The anonymous medieval text embodied in corrected fols. 163r–217r

reviewNotes: The medieval work, its sole manuscript witness, Todd's printed edition and apparatus, U-M's OCR and linked scans, independent scans of the edition, and the local XML are separate entities with separate access and rights statements.
Todd's old foliation and Jefferson's corrected foliation are a crosswalk for the same manuscript item, not evidence of two witnesses.
The enclosing TCD catalog title ‘John Wycliffe, tracts’ is legacy collection-level description; the same item record expressly says that the Apology incipit at fols. 164–218 is not by Wycliffe.
Negative public-facsimile findings are date-bounded to 2026-07-11 and do not prove that TCD has never digitized the manuscript or that no private reproduction exists.

lineage.summary: The repository XML is a transformed local copy/version of University of Michigan CME item ACM9160.0001.001, an uncorrected OCR/TEI Level 1 encoding of James Henthorn Todd's 1842 Camden Society edition. U-M encoded the title page and medieval text on printed pp. 1–113 while linking all 272 page images and leaving Todd's introduction, notes, glossary, and errata largely as page milestones only. Todd transcribed a single item in Trinity College Dublin MS 245 (legacy C.5.6): corrected fols. 163r–217r, corresponding to old fols. 164–218. Todd explicitly treated Wycliffe's authorship as uncertain; the current TCD item record says this exact item is not by Wycliffe.

catalogSummary.author: Anonymous
catalogSummary.date: Conventionally dated c. 1400; attested in a manuscript copied in the first half of the fifteenth century; first printed 1842
catalogSummary.region: England; the sole witness's scribal language is most likely Huntingdonshire in the southern Central Midlands
catalogSummary.title: An Apology for Lollard Doctrines

catalogingSubject.label: An Apology for Lollard Doctrines
catalogingSubject.scope_note: The anonymous Middle English prose defence of Lollard positions beginning ‘Here are questiouns and ansueris putte þat are writun here aftir,’ preserved as one item in Trinity College Dublin MS 245 and first printed under James Henthorn Todd's supplied title in 1842. This subject excludes the other works in the MS 245 miscellany, Todd's introduction, notes, glossary, and errata, the University of Michigan OCR/TEI representation, and the repository XML as distinct physical, editorial, digital, and local layers.

assertion.notes: The OED conventionally dates citations from the work c. 1400; the first-half-fifteenth-century sole witness supplies a secure upper bound but not an exact composition year.

notes: No normalized interval is supplied because the approximation is conventional and the author's identity and exact historical occasion remain unresolved.

assertion.notes: Jefferson dates MS 245 to s. xv¹; the current TCD catalog gives the broader fifteenth-century range 1400–1499.

dateStatements.display: First half of the fifteenth century
notes: This is the production date of the sole known witness, not an exact composition date.

assertion.notes: Todd's title page says ‘Now first printed,’ and Jefferson identifies his Camden Society volume as the edition from this manuscript.

extent.description: One prose work represented in the repository XML by one prologue plus 29 chapter DIV1 elements. The sequence contains a missing 27 and a likely duplicated/misprinted XIX before XXX. The 394 body NOTE1 elements are mostly short biblical, patristic, and canon-law references or summaries and are not counted as work divisions.

formStatements.description: A continuous prose defence organized as objections or questions followed by first-person answers.

assertion.notes: The first-person speaker answers charges or propositions and repeatedly offers correction by Scripture; Todd's modern title interprets this posture as an apology.

genres.label: Question-and-answer treatise
notes: This is an objection-and-response form, not a dramatic dialogue between separately identified speakers.

languageStatements.label: Middle English
notes: The U-M header's generic ‘eng’ label does not distinguish the medieval work's Middle English from Todd's modern English editorial matter.

languageStatements.label: Latin
notes: Latin is embedded and quoted, not a co-primary language of the work; Todd's extensive Latin apparatus belongs to the excluded edition layer.

scope.description: Short biblical, patristic, canon-law, and scholastic phrases and references embedded within the Middle English argument

notes: Todd's London publication place belongs to the 1842 edition and is not used as a medieval place of composition.
Todd's old foliation 164a–218a and the current catalog's fols. 164–218 correspond to Jefferson's corrected fols. 163r–217r because old foliation skipped 133; they are not different witnesses.
The sole manuscript is a composite Lollard miscellany containing 27 items in Jefferson's modern grouping; Todd's older description counted 29. Neither count is the number of chapters in the Apology.

next_steps: Consult the 2024 Index of Middle English Prose Handlist XXV entry for MS 245 and recent specialist work on this exact treatise.
Keep Wycliffe as a disputed historical attribution unless new direct authorship evidence supersedes the TCD record.
notes: The current record's c. 1400 date is conventional; the first-half-fifteenth-century witness is the secure terminus ante quem represented here.
openQuestions.question: Can current scholarship identify a narrower composition date, place, historical occasion, or author for the treatise?

next_steps: Compare Todd pp. 1–113 directly with images of corrected fols. 163r–217r if TCD supplies a reproduction.
Do not silently repair the missing 27 or duplicated XIX in work-level structure before that comparison.
openQuestions.question: Which chapter headings, marginal summaries, and anomalous numerals are original to MS 245, and which were supplied or normalized by Todd?

notes: England is the cultural and linguistic setting of this Middle English Lollard work; no exact place of composition is established.

assertion.notes: Jefferson's traditional analysis places the scribe in the southern half of the Central Midlands and her Atlas analysis finds Huntingdonshire most likely, with occasional more northerly forms possibly belonging to the exemplar or scribe.

notes: This localizes the manuscript scribe, not necessarily the author or place of composition.

scope.description: Scribal language of the sole MS 245 witness

assertion.notes: The current TCD item record expressly identifies this manuscript item as not by Wycliffe; no other named author is established by the evidence reviewed.

What is confirmed is the history and rejection of the attribution, not Wycliffe's authorship. Todd said there was no decisive reason to attribute the work to Wycliffe, and the current TCD item description says ‘Not by Wycliffe.’

notes: Do not promote the enclosing TCD catalog title ‘John Wycliffe, tracts,’ the U-M ‘supposed author’ field, or older library catalog author headings into accepted work-level authorship.

scope.description: Proof sheets checked against the sole manuscript, with assistance from Richard Gibbings

subjects.label: Religious poverty, orders, labor, and begging
Scripture, canon law, and patristic authority

summaries.value: An anonymous Middle English defence of positions associated with early Lollardy. After a prologue in which the first-person speaker submits every claim to Scripture and correction, the treatise responds to objections concerning papal and clerical authority, indulgences, excommunication, preaching and priestly ministry, the Mass and sacraments, poverty, fasting and prayer, churches and images, simony and Antichrist, marriage and canon law, discipleship, vows, charms, religious labor, and begging. Its method repeatedly tests ecclesiastical practice against Scripture, patristic authorities, and canon law.

Todd deliberately presented Wycliffe's authorship as an open question: he found the work compatible with early Wycliffite thought but admitted that its occurrence among attributed Wycliffite tracts was the only positive evidence and that there was no decisive reason to assign it to Wycliffe. The current TCD item record now labels the exact incipit and folio range ‘Not by Wycliffe’; this record therefore catalogs the author as anonymous and preserves Wycliffe only as a disputed historical attribution.

The repository encoding has one prologue and 29 chapter divisions. Its chapter attributes run 1–26, 28, 19, 30: there is no encoded chapter 27, and the penultimate heading is printed ‘XIX’ where sequence suggests ‘XXIX.’ Because Todd acknowledges adding some marginal summaries and references, this record reports the encoded/printed divisions without treating every heading or numeral as an uncontested authorial structure.

notes: Todd states that this title has no authority from the manuscript; it is retained as the established editorial and scholarly title.
‘Attributed to Wicliffe’ records Todd's qualified historical attribution; it is not a statement of accepted authorship.
titles.value: An Apology for Lollard Doctrines, Attributed to Wicliffe

notes: The TCD catalog and Jefferson use this opening to identify the otherwise untitled manuscript item.
titles.value: Here are questiouns and ansueris putte þat are writun here aftir

work.author: Anonymous
work.dateDisplay: Conventionally dated c. 1400; attested in a manuscript copied in the first half of the fifteenth century; first printed 1842

publication.embeddedTitle: AN APOLOGY FOR LOLLARD DOCTRINES, — ATTRIBUTED TO WICLIFFE.

work.regionDisplay: England; the sole witness's scribal language is most likely Huntingdonshire in the southern Central Midlands

subjects.label: Religious poverty, orders, labor, and begging
Scripture, canon law, and patristic authority

work.summary: An anonymous Middle English defence of positions associated with early Lollardy. After a prologue in which the first-person speaker submits every claim to Scripture and correction, the treatise responds to objections concerning papal and clerical authority, indulgences, excommunication, preaching and priestly ministry, the Mass and sacraments, poverty, fasting and prayer, churches and images, simony and Antichrist, marriage and canon law, discipleship, vows, charms, religious labor, and begging. Its method repeatedly tests ecclesiastical practice against Scripture, patristic authorities, and canon law.
